coredata.dc:title: Automated electrochemical impedance spectroscopy fitting for quantitative analysis of degradation modes in lithium-ion batteries

coredata.dc:description: Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) is a powerful diagnostic technique for probing internal electrochemical processes and assessing lithium-ion battery degradation, yet its practical application is often limited by the complexity and subjectivity of manual fitting procedures. This paper proposes a fully automated algorithm for extracting equivalent circuit model (ECM) parameters from EIS measurements to quantify the battery degradation modes, namely conductivity loss (CL), loss of active material (LAM), and loss of lithium inventory (LLI). The developed method integrates an automated partitioning of the measured impedance spectrum into three frequency regions using optimized percentile thresholds. This segmentation enables an initial estimation of circuit parameters describing lithium-ion cell behaviour, including ohmic resistance, charge-transfer resistances, non-ideal capacitances, and Warburg diffusion elements. These initial estimates are subsequently refined through an iterative, sequential optimization process based on a Trust Region Reflective least-squares algorithm, using a reference spectrum and propagating the optimized parameters across successive aging cycles. The algorithm was validated using two experimental datasets comprising various cell types and impedance magnitudes ranging from micro-ohms to a few ohms. The proposed approach minimizes operator intervention and provides a reliable and scalable tool for battery health monitoring, suitable for both real-life diagnostics and research activities involving large volumes of experimental data.

head.ce:title: Automated electrochemical impedance spectroscopy fitting for quantitative analysis of degradation modes in lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Consiglio Nazionale delle Ricerche Istituto di Tecnologie Avanzate per l'Energia, Salita Santa Lucia, 5, Messina, 98126, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) is a powerful diagnostic technique for probing internal electrochemical processes and assessing lithium-ion battery degradation, yet its practical application is often limited by the complexity and subjectivity of manual fitting procedures. This paper proposes a fully automated algorithm for extracting equivalent circuit model (ECM) parameters from EIS measurements to quantify the battery degradation modes, namely conductivity loss (CL), loss of active material (LAM), and loss of lithium inventory (LLI). The developed method integrates an automated partitioning of the measured impedance spectrum into three frequency regions using optimized percentile thresholds. This segmentation enables an initial estimation of circuit parameters describing lithium-ion cell behaviour, including ohmic resistance, charge-transfer resistances, non-ideal capacitances, and Warburg diffusion elements. These initial estimates are subsequently refined through an iterative, sequential optimization process based on a Trust Region Reflective least-squares algorithm, using a reference spectrum and propagating the optimized parameters across successive aging cycles. The algorithm was validated using two experimental datasets comprising various cell types and impedance magnitudes ranging from micro-ohms to a few ohms. The proposed approach minimizes operator intervention and provides a reliable and scalable tool for battery health monitoring, suitable for both real-life diagnostics and research activities involving large volumes of experimental data.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data are online available at https://doi.org/10.17632/g7ncmhrcrz.1

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid development of lithium-ion batteries has revolutionized energy storage, with applications ranging from mobile phones to electric vehicles. These batteries play a crucial role in supporting the transition to renewable energy, reducing dependence on fossil fuels, and lowering greenhouse gas emissions [1,2]. However, to fully exploit their potential, it is essential to understand the factors affecting their performance and longevity. Key factors influencing battery lifetime include cell and electrode design, such as chemistry, shape, porosity, capacity, and additives, which impact phase transformations and side reactions [3,4]. Additionally, operating conditions significantly affect battery degradation. High temperatures and self-heating can cause irreversible phase changes in cathode materials, electrolyte decomposition, and rapid growth of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) on the negative electrode [5,6]. Fast or pulsed charge/discharge rates exacerbate side reactions, including SEI growth, binder decomposition, electrolyte oxidation, and active material particle cracking [7,8]. Furthermore, the voltage window during cycling influences degradation by affecting capacity loss and internal impedance. Wide voltage windows and the resulting wide depth of discharge (DoD) lead to more severe aging in batteries [9]. These degradation processes contribute to three primary forms of degradation modes in lithium-ion batteries namely: Conductivity Loss (CL) being the increase of internal resistance due to degradation of parts of the battery such as current collector corrosion or binder decomposition; Loss of Active Material (LAM) being the irreversible loss of electrode material due to mechanical or chemical breakdown; Loss of Lithium Inventory (LLI) being the reduction in available lithium ions, which can result from side reactions such as SEI formation and lithium plating [10]. Quantifying CL, LAM, and LLI is essential for understanding batteries degradation, then improving the design and extending their life. Conducting a comprehensive post-mortem analysis of aged lithium-ion batteries is a valuable technique to identify the key degradation mechanisms, including the loss of active material and loss lithium inventory [11]. However, this method involves disassembling the battery after its end of life and examining the physical and chemical changes in the electrode materials. Therefore, while post-mortem analysis provides valuable insights into the degradation mechanisms, its destructive nature limits its applicability for ongoing battery management and predictive maintenance. In contrast, non-invasive diagnostic techniques have been widely reported in the literature for their effectiveness in characterizing and quantifying these degradation mechanisms, making them more suitable for real-time monitoring and practical applications [11]. Differential voltage analysis (DVA) and incremental capacity analysis (ICA) are diagnostic techniques that have been extensively used to study battery degradation. These methods involve analysing the differential or incremental capacity curves obtained during charge and discharge cycles. The shift and change in these curves can be used to determine CL, LAM and LLI [12]. To facilitate the analysis of degradation modes in lithium-ion cells using DVA or ICA, Dahn et al. [13] and Dubarry et al. [14,15] have also developed and released specialized software toolboxes.

Another non-invasive technique that can be used to analyse the degradation of a lithium-ion battery under various operating conditions is the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). This technique has recently been recognized as a valuable tool for the rapid and accurate estimation of the State of Health (SoH). New approaches focus on directly linking variations in ohmic and charge-transfer parameters to the evolution of residual capacity across various cell chemistries [16–19]. Furthermore, EIS enables to investigate the internal properties of lithium-ion batteries by fitting the impedance data to equivalent circuit models (ECMs), which parameter can be used to identify and quantify the main degradation modes. Pastor-Fernández et al. [20] established a relationship between the changes in resistance of a second-order equivalent circuit model and each degradation mode, providing a framework for identifying and quantifying aging mechanisms using EIS. To validate their model, they also compared ICA-DVA and EIS analysis on the same data set demonstrating that both techniques provide similar insights into the causes of battery degradation [21]. Then, Sun et al. [22] demonstrated the use of EIS combined with equivalent circuit models to quantify the degradation modes of lithium-ion batteries under different operating conditions, highlighting the critical role of LLI and LAM in battery aging. Similarly, Teliz et al. proposed the use of the mentioned method to identify and quantify the aging mechanisms in commercial 18,650 NMC lithium-ion batteries over time using the EIS technique [23]. Therefore, it has been clearly demonstrated that EIS can offer several distinct advantages over other diagnostic techniques. Unlike ICA and DVA, which primarily focus on changes of quasi-OCV curves and require extensive time for data acquisition, EIS provides a comprehensive analysis of the battery's internal electrochemical processes in a relatively faster measurement. Furthermore, EIS can be easily integrated into Battery Management Systems (BMS), enabling non-invasive online analysis and diagnostics during normal operation [24]. These advancements highlight the need for robust analytical tools that can translate raw impedance data into actionable health indicators.

Although, it has been recognized that EIS is a powerful diagnostic tool for characterizing battery aging, its application is often hindered by the complexity and subjectivity of data interpretation. Traditional methods for fitting EIS data to ECMs require extensive expertise and can be time-consuming [25–27]. The most commonly way for fitting EIS data is the use of dedicated tools integrated in owner software packages [28–33]. However, these software packages do not allow for the simultaneous fitting of multiple spectra with an overarching logic, meaning each spectrum must be analysed independently. This significantly contributes to the time-consuming nature of the process and increases the likelihood that the results may not follow a consistent trend.

Additionally, the fitting of EIS data to ECMs is particularly problematic due to the need for accurate initial parameter estimation, which is often dependent on the operator's experience and can significantly affect the results [34]. To address these challenges, recent Researchers have focused on developing more automated and reliable methods for EIS data interpretation. For instance, Zhang et al. [35] introduced an open-source tool named “AutoEIS” that assists EIS analysis by automatically proposing statistically plausible equivalent circuit models (ECMs). However, since it is not based on mechanistic understanding of the electrochemical systems, the physics of the phenomenon could be lost unless the results are analysed by an expert operator. Furthermore, the tool could require significant computational resources that are not ideal for practical applications. A tool designed to make the analysis of EIS data easier and more reproducible has been also proposed by Murbach et al. [36]. Although it offers a user-friendly interface and a range of functionalities, it still requires the definition of a circuit and the accurate initial parameter estimation, which can be challenging without sufficient expertise. A more interesting methodology has been recently proposed by Santoni et al. [37]. They provide a detailed guide for fitting EIS data by equivalent circuit models (ECMs), which involves a comprehensive method for estimating initial values of the optimization algorithm, finalized to estimate state of charge (SOC) and state of health (SOH) of lithium-ion batteries. Beyond standard equivalent circuit models, recent literature has explored advanced techniques such as the Distribution of Relaxation Times (DRT) to enhance the resolution of electrochemical processes, although these methods often require complex deconvolution algorithms that can hinder large-scale automation [38]. In this context, there is a growing demand for ECM fitting tools that are both physically consistent and computationally efficient to handle the vast datasets generated by long-term aging tests and second-life applications [39].

In this work, the utility of a simple and ready-to-use lithium-ion battery diagnostic tool based on Python code has been combined with an automated EIS data fitting methodology, specifically calibrated on real battery data, to track the evolution of the main degradation mechanisms with aging. The proposed method introduces a significant simplification in the determination of the initial parameters of the ECM. These are calculated only once on an EIS spectrum used as reference, refined by an optimisation algorithm, and then reused as initial parameters for subsequent fitting of other EIS spectra. This approach reduces the dependency on operator expertise, improves the accuracy of the fitting and decreases the time required for data processing. Additionally, a data analysis model to estimate LAM, LLI and CL based on the evolution of equivalent circuit parameters has been included in the tool. The method has been developed and validated on multiple lithium-ion cell data sets containing several aging conditions (e.g., different C-rates, temperatures, etc.) and various chemistries, enhancing its robustness and applicability across different battery types. Finally, it is presented a test-case application using an EIS dataset of eight commercial 18,650 NCA cells aged under different operating conditions. This case study demonstrates the capability of the tool for accurately quantifying the three main degradation modes of the aged lithium-ion cells, further highlighting its potential as a comprehensive diagnostic solution.
With the purpose of extracting information from EIS measurement data to be correlated with degradation of lithium-ion cell, circuital theory and existing EIS-based aging models were considered.

The first part of this work was to find the best possible circuit to represent EIS experimental data with the right accuracy while being a simple mathematical equation able to be implemented in a code and to run with a high number of input data. Practically, this means to find the parameters of the equivalent circuit model able to reproduce the behaviour of the battery. This is a regression process starting from the estimation of suitable initial values for each parameter followed by their optimization. The initial parameters are calculated and optimized only once on the first EIS spectrum analysed, while these optimized parameters are made available as initial conditions for fitting of other correlated EIS spectra in the dataset.

The second part of this work was the implementation of a lithium-ion battery aging model to correlate EIS data to the main degradation modes LAM, LLI and CL, using circuital parameters extrapolated by the fitting process.

Optimization of the developed algorithm has been performed on an experimental dataset developed during this work, comprising different EIS spectra recorded for a lithium-ion cell aged under different conditions. The validation is then carried out onto multiple owned and open-access data, as described in section 3. To achieve an automated tool for parameters extraction a Python code based on Pandas (data set management), NumPy and SciPy (implementation of numerical methods) has been developed. An example of the developed code is provided in Annex S1.

While it is possible to combine EIS measurements with detailed physical models that describe every process in the battery, such models are generally too complex for practical applications. Instead, simplified equivalent circuit models (ECMs) are used to capture the overall impedance behaviour [40]. These models rely on circuits composed of basic elements such as resistors, capacitors, and inductors, as well as generalized electrical components, which mathematical equation is used to fit the data. The most generalized ECM is directly derived from the Newman's model which describes mathematically the electrochemistry of porous electrodes. However, in practical applications, this complex model can be simplified and reduced to the well-known De Levie model or further simplified to a Randles-like equivalent circuit [41]. Despite this simplification, they can ensure the approximation of EIS spectra at the initial life of the cell. Moreover, the electrochemical properties of the battery components change with cycling number, SoC and temperature, then leading to variations in the impedance spectra. The use of an equivalent circuit model with parameters that can adapt to these changes, it is possible to accurately fit the EIS data and monitor their aging process [42]. For instance, while a simple first-order Randles circuit is often sufficient to describe the impedance of a fresh cell, the aging process of lithium-ion batteries typically leads to the emergence of new or more pronounced electrochemical phenomena. The first semicircle, appearing at mid-to-high frequencies, is commonly associated with ion migration through the SEI layer, while the second semicircle, at mid-to-low frequencies, is linked to the charge-transfer resistance [43]. Therefore, a second-order model, featuring two distinct time constants is therefore particularly well-suited to decouple and individually track these processes. This approach provides a meaningful balance between model simplicity and physical representativeness, and it is widely adopted for monitoring the degradation of commercial lithium-ion cells.

Based on this, two common second-order equivalent circuits are proposed to fit the impedance of lithium-ion cells [41], namely Circuit A and Circuit B as shown in Fig. 1 . Circuit A is composed of one inductor in series with a resistor together with two Randles-like branches; one containing a parallel of non-ideal capacitor and a resistance, the other with a Warburg element included. Circuit B is the same as the previous one, but the Warburg impedance due to solid-state diffusion is removed from the second Randles-like branch and placed in series with the two parallels. Both circuits have nine characteristic parameters including resistance (Rohm, Rct1, Rct2), inductance (L), Warburg (W) and constant phase elements (CPE1, CPE2). Each CPE is then composed of two parameters, respectively a generalized capacitance (Q) and an exponent (α) that characterizes the non-ideality of the capacitor (where α = 1 leads to the case of an ideal capacitor and α = 0.5 is the special case representative of the Warburg element).

Since the behaviour of electrochemical systems including batteries, can be characterized in terms of a generalized Ohm's law, their impedance can be represented by the Laplace transform as [44,45]:

The EIS fitting algorithm finds the best nine parameters for the equivalent circuit to capture how impedance changes as the battery ages. It does this by correlating these parameters with experimental EIS data, starting with an initial guess and then iteratively optimizing them at each aging cycle.

The proper determination of these parameters for each individual spectroscopy is achieved through a least squares algorithm, based on a Trust Region Reflective (TRF) optimization method, performed by SciPy.optimize algorithm included in SciPy Python library. TRF is used to minimize the sum of squares of nonlinear functions, being more stable compared to gradient-based method, especially in the presence of highly nonlinear functions [46].

Under the assumption that the dataset includes a family of EIS spectra recorded at various aging cycles and grouped for a same SOC (see schematic structure in Fig. 2 and a real dataset in Fig. S1), here it is proposed to fit the spectra per each SOC starting from an initial EIS spectrum used as a reference. As will be discussed below, for the final version of the fitting algorithm, the reference EIS spectrum has been selected as the most aged condition available for each SOC.

Fig. 3 shows a detailed flowchart of the fitting algorithm. Starting from the reference EIS spectrum in the dataset, the code will first estimate the nine initial parameters of the ECM. It will then find their optimized values to best fit the spectrum by iteratively using the least squares algorithm. The optimized parameters resulting from the fitting of the first spectrum are then used as initial conditions to optimize the fit of the second spectrum. This process is repeated until all the spectra belonging to a SOC have been fitted. At the end of the process, the code returns the optimized circuit parameters for each aging cycle, alongside the predicted real and imaginary data for each impedance spectrum.

The use of equivalent circuits considered in Fig. 1 allows to correlate with good approximation its parameters with the main phenomena involved in the electrochemical process of a lithium-ion cell. Indeed, since the rate of movement of charges through each electrochemical interface differs, it is possible to represent each interface as a part of the circuit with a specific time constant. Therefore, it is possible to decouple the various processes by considering the frequency range in which their impedance is dominant [47]. At high frequencies (from Hz to tens of kHz magnitude), the impedance of fast processes, such as electron movements in the electrodes and ion movements in the electrolyte mass, is dominant. At medium frequencies (few Hz magnitude), ion transport through a permeable passivation layer (solid electrolyte interphase - SEI) and charge transfer phenomena are of primary importance. Finally, at low frequencies of few mHz, solid-state diffusion of ions is the primary phenomenon.

A typical EIS spectrum of a lithium-ion cell spans from tens of kHz to a few mHz on a logarithmic scale [48]. In this work, the frequency range is divided into three region, namely high, mid, and low frequency, using two percentile thresholds calculated with Python's NumPy library. The optimal percentile thresholds ( P ) of this function have been set to 50 and 90, respectively.
P 50
represents the midpoint of the dataset, below which 50% of the data points fall.
P 90
indicates the point below which 90% of the data points fall. Then the frequency regions are defined as follows.

These percentile thresholds were empirically determined during code calibration on a diverse set of EIS spectra covering multiple lithium-ion chemistries, cell formats, SOC levels, and aging states. It should also be emphasized that with the proposed percentile approach, the frequency range partitioning is entirely automatic, and no manual tuning is required. This fully automated procedure yields a reproducible, operator-free segmentation into high, mid, and low frequency regions.

A schematic representation of the three regions in a typical Nyquist plot is depicted in Fig. 4 . In high frequency region, the impedance is predominantly influenced by inductive effects and the ohmic resistance.
P 90
is chosen to ensure that the analysis focuses on the high-frequency tail of the spectrum, capturing the inductance and the ohmic resistance with minimal interference from other components. Mid frequency region between
P 90
and
P 50
is ideal for capturing the characteristics of the SEI impedance. The
P 50
ensures that these parameters are estimated based on data points that represent a balanced mix of influences from both high and low frequencies. Furthermore, by using the
P 50
percentile, the analysis encompasses a significant portion of the low frequency range, providing robust initial estimates for charge transfer impedance and Warburg diffusion. In this work, a such approach has been tested within the typical logarithmic distribution of frequency ranges used for EIS spectra of lithium-ion cells spanning from tens of kHz to few mHz. It was highlighting that the proposed thresholds
P 90
and
P 50
give a significative partition of data according to the schematic of Fig. 4. However, it must be noted that using different distributions of data in the frequency range, the number of points above
P 90
and below
P 50
can change significantly, and therefore the proposed thresholds could need adjustments to be tuned with the experimental data.

In this study, the least squares algorithm is employed to optimize the parameters of the equivalent circuit model. As first, this method requires the definition of bounds within which the variables can be found. The lower bound is set to zero to maintain the physical meaning of the variables, while the upper bound varies depending on the estimated parameter values. Initially, arbitrary values are assigned to the upper bounds, which are updated if the estimated values exceed their bound. This algorithm is implemented in the code to find the optimal curve that minimizes the sum of squared errors ( S ) tuning the circuit parameters with the TRF optimization method. The errors (
ε i
) are defined as the difference between the experimental values
Z i
and the values obtained from the fitting
Z ̂
i
. These differences are then squared and summed for all points on the curve and therefore the function to be minimized is:

where n is the total number of experimental points in one spectroscopy measurement. By minimizing this sum, the least squares method finds the optimal parameters of the model that best approximates the experimental data. This approach is widely used in various scientific and engineering fields to construct predictive models based on experimental data [51].

To identify an optimal fit and ensuring the process accurately determines a set of parameters that provides a better representation of the experimental data, the developed method makes use of the Chi-squared (
χ 2
), which is expressed as:

The quality of experimental EIS data can be affected by many factors (e.g. setup configuration, instrument accuracy and operator experience) and poor data quality may affect the accuracy of parameter identification. The input EIS datasets adopted in this work originate from reliable experimental campaigns where conditions ensuring data quality, such as system linearity, stability and causality were verified through a preliminary Kramers-Kronig test carried out using a commercial fitting tool (NOVA-Metrohm AG). Therefore, the data were considered suitable for direct fitting. However, for applications involving data from unknown sources, a data quality pre-processing checks is mandatory. In this case, the open code released with this work could be readily customized to include a preliminary Kramers-Kronig test to validate the quality of the input spectra.

In systems with series RC circuits, an additional challenge is ensuring that the fitted parameters have a meaningful physical interpretation. Parallel RC circuits in series can be arbitrarily swapped during fitting, leading to ambiguities in their physical significance. To address this, a verification step based on the following time constant has been introduced:
(33)
τ =
R ct
Q
1 α

This parameter is critical in determining the speed of processes within the circuit, with larger time constants typically associated with slower phenomena. The method involves reordering the parallel RC circuits post-fitting to ensure that they follow a logical sequence: the first parallel RC circuit corresponds to faster processes (smaller time constant), and the second to slower processes (larger time constant). This step not only preserves the physical meaning of the fitted parameters but also ensures consistency across different fittings and datasets.
The algorithm employed for the identification of the aging phenomena affecting the lithium-ion cells under analysis was adopted from the model proposed by Pastor-Fernández et al. [21].

This mathematical model quantifies the three main aging phenomena (CL, LAM and LLI) by analysing the evolution of the EIS impedance spectra with the number of aging cycles at specific SOC levels. This quantification is achieved by correlating each phenomenon with the parameters of the equivalent circuits depicted in Fig. 1. It should be noted that, although the equivalent circuits are mathematically represented by nine parameters, the proposed aging model employs only four of these, specifically those representing the real part of the impedance (
R Ohm
,
R
ct 1
,
R
ct 2
) , and Warburg diffusion (
z w
) . The model assumes that the resistive parameters of the equivalent circuit are mainly related to electrochemical phenomena of the cell, thereby making them suitable for representing the three main aging phenomena [20].

R Ohm
models the resistance in current collectors, binder and electrolytes. Alteration of these components lead to conductivity loss, manifested as voltage drop through the cell. Therefore, an increase in
R Ohm
is representative of CL aging. The intercalation and de-intercalation of lithium-ions between the anode and the cathode causes the build-up of the SEI layer, which leads to irreversible consumption of usable lithium. This results in notable capacity fade and increased resistance. Therefore, the rise in SEI resistance (
R
ct 1
) is a direct consequence of LLI. Besides SEI, other degradation effects like dendrite growth and micro-pore clogging, primarily attributed to LLI, also play a role. These effects, along with SEI, reduce the efficiency of charge-transfer reactions during lithium-ion intercalation and de-intercalation, leading to an increase in
R
ct 2
. Consequently,
R
ct 2
increases as a result of LLI, indicating that LLI influences both
R
ct 1
and
R
ct 2
.

The diffusion process of lithium ions through the porous electrodes, which is modeled by the Warburg impedance, depends on the effective diffusion path length. Morphological changes and structural transformations of the porous electrodes, altering the lithium ions diffusion path, allows to directly correlate the increase of the Warburg resistance (
z w
) to the LAM aging mechanism. Therefore, the quantification of CL, LAM and LLI is carried out by calculating the percentage variation of each of the four circuital parameters of interest. In particular, the proposed model allows to quantify each one of them at different aging cycles (k) for each SOC group (q), according to the following equations, where the variation of each of the four parameters is referred to its initial state ( k = 0 ).
(34)
CL k q

It is worth noting that the Warburg-based LAM represents the total loss of active material in the full cell, i.e. the combined effect of the positive and negative electrodes. Recent research has demonstrated that differential techniques such as DVA and IC, when applied to a full-cell configuration, could enable the degradation of active materials at the positive (LAMPE) and negative (LAMNE) electrodes to be separated. However, this may require complex models based on combinations of many parameters and the knowledge of specific half-cell quasi-OCV curves to fit the experimental data more accurately [52]. The extension of the EIS-based model proposed, while retaining the ECM and the automation algorithm, could still be exploited to extract LAMPE and LAMNE although it would require the use of a half-cell impedance data.

The proposed methodology is implemented with the development of a Python code optimized and tuned with a series of tests performed on experimental EIS data available in owned and online public repositories. More in detail, the Dataset 1, which contains EIS data of a lithium-ion cell aged under different conditions, has been created from a dedicated experimental test campaign (see Section 3.1.1). Dataset 2 has been assembled organizing owned and public EIS data coming from different lithium-ion cells (see Section 3.1.2). Then, the process to develop the code was divided in Calibration, Validation and Demonstration test case as per Fig. 5 . The code development has been carried out on a workstation equipped with an Intel(R) Core (TM) i9 13th Gen 2.60 GHz CPU and 64 GB RAM.

This dataset, consisting of 500 EIS spectra, is the result of a dedicated experimental test campaign carried out for the purpose of this work and online available [53]. Eight cylindrical lithium-ion cells (model INR18650-30Q manufactured by Samsung SDI Co., Ltd.) with a nominal capacity of 2.95 Ah have been aged under four different operating conditions up to 80% of their initial capacity. Capacity fade with aging cycles of each cell is reported in Fig. S2. Two identical cells were aged for each of the four Aging Profiles (AP). AP1 performed at ambient temperature of 23 °C, is a cycle aging that includes charging at constant current (CC) of 4 A and constant voltage (CV) at 4.2 V up to 150 mA cut-off, followed by discharging at 15 A to 2.5 V, with 10-min rest periods between charge and discharge and 30 min after discharge. AP2 had a similar profile to AP1 but used a constant current of 5 A for charging instead of 4 A. AP3 profile was the same as AP1 with a higher ambient temperature of 45 °C. The AP4 profile was carried out under the same conditions as AP1, but with a reduced depth of charge, eliminating the CV charging phase.

Periodic checkups were carried out for fresh cells and every 25 aging cycles. These included capacity and impedance measurements recorded at 23 °C in accordance with the optimum conditions recommended by the cell manufacturer. EIS data were recorded at five SOC levels (100–75–50-25-0%) and for different aging states, in the frequency range 10 kHz to 100 mHz, with 10 points per decade. Testing has been conducted using an Arbin LBT 21044 Battery Test System, equipped with a Gamry 5000E potentiostat and an Arbin MZTC climate chamber for temperature control across eight compartments. A summary of Dataset 1 with aging conditions for each cell and related number of EIS spectra is presented in Table 1 .

This dataset, consisting of 76 spectra, is built collecting data from owned and public repositories. A total of six lithium-ion cells having different chemistry and capacity have been selected and organized in a single dataset consisting of: Cell A (cylindrical 60,280 LFP cell, manufactured by Shenzhen Yhd Electronic Co having a nominal capacity of 80 Ah) – one EIS measurement at 25 °C and 100% SOC with 51 points in the frequency range from 1000 Hz to 0.01 Hz recorded at fresh state; Cell B (pouch NMC cell, model SLPB 140460330 manufactured by Kokam, having a nominal capacity of 200 Ah) – one EIS measurement at 25 °C and 100% SOC with 51 points in the frequency range from 1000 to 0.01 Hz recorded at fresh state; Cell C (cylindrical 18,650 NCA cell, model 35E manufactured by Samsung SDI Co., Ltd. having a nominal capacity of 3350 mAh) – sixty EIS measurements at 25 °C for different SOC (100–75–50-25-0%) with 50 points in the frequency range from 794 Hz to 0.01 Hz recorded at fresh and aged states; Cell D (pouch LiPo cell, model LP-503562-IS-3 manufactured by BAK Technology having a nominal capacity of 1.05 Ah) – thirteen EIS measurements at 25 °C and 100% SOC with 45 points in the frequency range from 5000 Hz to 0.2 Hz recorded at fresh and aged state (LiPO5 from [54]); Cell E (coin 2032 LCO/graphite cells manufactured by EUNICELL with a nominal capacity of 45 mAh) – one EIS measurement at 25 °C and 100% SOC with 60 points in the frequency range from 20,000 Hz to 0.02 Hz recorded at fresh state [55]. A summary of Dataset 2 with aging states for each cell and related number of EIS spectra is presented in Table 2 . Aging of Cell C consisted of 450 standard cycles (0.3C CC-CV charge and 1C discharge) at 25 °C up to about 10% capacity loss. The aging profile for Cell D is detailed in [54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration, validation and demonstration of the code
ce:section.ce:para: The code calibration phase consisted in tuning the fundamental parameters of the algorithm to make it as much as possible suitable for different situations. For this purpose, a strategy is proposed to automatically estimate the initial parameters for each initial spectrum to ensure the convergence of the subsequent least squares algorithm. This approach is based on the analysis of impedance spectrum at three frequency ranges with the main objective of extrapolating the correct order of magnitude for the circuit parameters. To validate this approach different EIS spectra of the fresh cells contained in Dataset 1 and Dataset 2 have been considered. Furthermore, the calibration phase included the optimization of the code in terms of identifying the best circuit model to be used and the evaluation of the EIS fitting sequence to better follow the aging evolution of the parameters. Both optimizations were carried out on EIS spectra coming from Dataset 1.

The code validation has been carried out with several EIS data to test the accuracy of the algorithm in estimating the evolution of the fitting parameters as the impedance changes, and thus with the cells aging. Experimental EIS data considered came from both the Dataset 1 and Dataset 2. In particular, the analysis of Dataset 1 allowed to test the complete automaticity of the code for fitting a whole dataset of EIS spectra recorded at different aging levels and for different SOCs. In addition, the EIS spectra of cell C from Dataset 2 were used to verify the accuracy of the proposed fitting methodology for a set of impedance data characterized by a limited frequency range and irregular evolution with aging. The validation has been concluded, using Cell D from Dataset 2, with a comparison of the results of the proposed code with a similar method descripted in [37].

After proving the robustness of the proposed code, a test case application based on the Dataset 1 has been presented to demonstrate its use for quantifying the aging mechanisms (CL, LAM and LLI) of differently aged lithium-ion cells.

The proposed approach involves an iterative process where initial parameter estimations are continuously updated with optimized values until goodness of fit criteria are met. In fact, one of the most critical challenges of curve fitting is the choice of initial parameter values. Poor initial estimates can lead to suboptimal fits or failure to converge towards local minima. The methodology proposed in this work includes the calculation of the initial parameters of the equivalent circuit in three frequency ranges, which were identified by dividing the impedance data by a percentile-based approach descripted in Section 2.2.1. A fine tuning of the percentile thresholds (P) has been preliminary performed on EIS spectra coming from Dataset 1 and Dataset 2. The EIS spectra have been selected to cover different type of lithium-ion cells and different aging conditions, with impedance data ranging from a few milliohms, typical of large format cells, to several ohms, typical of coin and laboratory test cells. Furthermore, the EIS spectra varied in terms of their frequency ranges and the number of data points, reflecting the diversity of experimental conditions.

The frequency range of each EIS spectrum has been systematically divided into three regions: high frequency, mid frequency, and low frequency. Then, an optimal division has been achieved using percentile thresholds
P 50
and
P 90
. Table 3 shows the segmentation of frequency ranges determined using the above optimal percentile thresholds for six different EIS Spectra representative of a wide variety of experimental cases. Despite differences in the absolute frequency ranges and data point distributions, the adopted percentile thresholds consistently segmented the spectra into meaningful frequency regions that facilitated the extraction of initial parameter estimates that are crucial for the fitting algorithm.

Fig. 6 illustrates the fitting results for each EIS spectrum, highlighting the excellent agreement between the experimental data and the fitted models. This suggests that the proposed method can identify properly the initial parameters and hence it is able to fit different shape of EIS, typical of various lithium-ion cell technologies and experimental conditions.

For instance, the model can effectively fit the EIS spectra of both Cell A and Cell B, which are representative of low impedance cases with a large inductive behaviour, typical of large format cells [56]. It can be observed that the significant inductive part that is also properly represented by the proposed algorithm.

The method can also fit the EIS spectra of cells C, D, and Cell 5, which are representative of typical commercial cylindrical lithium-ion cells with capacities between 1 and 4 Ah and internal impedance in the order of tens to hundreds of milliohms [3,57]. Notably, the spectra of Cells 5 display only semicircular shapes without a low-frequency tail. This indicates that while diffusion phenomena are present, they are shifted to a lower frequency region not captured by the EIS measurements. Despite this, the method still provides an excellent fit to the experimental data.

Finally, since Cell E is an LR2032 coin type, its EIS spectrum is representative of small-format cells with high internal impedance of a few Ohm [58]. The initial parameter estimation method effectively identifies these impedance magnitudes, thereby facilitating the subsequent optimal fitting. This demonstrates the robustness of the method and its versatility across various cell types and impedance characteristics.

Consistency of the estimation method of initial parameters is also quantitatively demonstrated by the analysis of EIS fitting metrics reported in Table 4 . Low RMSE and high
R 2
values across all cells investigated, indicating high-quality fits regardless of the specific characteristics of the data.

The selection of the equivalent circuit models is a critical step in accurately fitting the EIS data and then, in monitoring the aging process of the lithium-ion cells. The two equivalent circuits reported in Fig. 1, namely Circuit A and Circuit B, were proposed for this purpose. Fig. 7a and b show the Nyquist plots of impedance recorded at five SOC levels for Cell 1 in the fresh state, and the respective fittings with both circuits. Both circuits have been found suitable to fit well mid and high SOC levels. However, at very low SOC levels, especially close to 0%, the fitting quality for the diffusive part is less accurate. This issue arises because, under these conditions, the diffusion process deviates from the behaviour modeled by the Warburg element, which assumes a semi-infinite diffusion process showing an oblique line with a slope of 45° in the Nyquist plot. Therefore, to accurately model low SOC levels, in particular, at low frequencies, it would be necessary to consider more complex circuits that use finite Warburg elements [59]. Nevertheless, the use of Circuit A or Circuit B does not show significant differences in the overall fitting quality.

To further assess and compare the performance of the two equivalent circuits, EIS spectra at different aging states have been analysed, then average
RMSE ¯
and
R 2
¯
values expressed according to (31) and (32), have been used for a quantitative evaluation. As shown in Fig. 8 , Circuit B demonstrated slightly lower
RMSE ¯
values and higher
R 2
¯
values compared to Circuit A across all SOC levels, indicating a better fit and greater accuracy in modelling the EIS data.

The correct placement of the Warburg impedance in equivalent circuit models for electrochemical systems has been discussed in [60]. In most traditional models, such as the Randles circuit, the Warburg element is placed in parallel with the charge-transfer resistance and double-layer capacitance. From a physical point of view, this solution allows for a more accurate representation of the diffusion-controlled impedance, which occurs simultaneously with the interfacial charge transfer processes. On the other hand, most of the equivalent circuits reported in the recent literature [61–63,27] use the diffusion element in series to the rest of the elements, similarly as in Circuit B. Indeed, while the change of Warburg element position in the circuit may be physically unrepresentative, in many cases, this repositioning can improve the quality of the fit. Therefore, when the study of transport phenomena is not the main research focus, the use of simplified Randles schemes can be recommended [60]. Based on the better accuracy observed using Circuit B, this solution is adopted in this work. However, it is worth noting that although both solutions are valid, depending on the experimental data the type of analysis, Circuit A may become a more suitable solution.

The proposed algorithm, which is designed for the fitting of a series of EIS spectra that change over time as the cell ages, involves estimating and optimizing the initial value of the chosen equivalent circuit model starting from one reference spectrum. The subsequent spectra are then fitted using the optimized parameters from the preceding spectrum as the initial input. This intuitive approach allows for a better tracking of the evolution of circuit parameters over time, eliminating the need to estimate the initial parameters for each EIS spectrum, which can introduce additional errors. Therefore, to evaluate the evolution of the circuit parameters with aging, it is possible the fitting of EIS spectra from a fresh cell to an aged cell (forward path) or from an aged cell to a fresh cell (reverse path). In this section the difference between the fitting by forward or reverse path has been compared.

Fig. 9a presents the Nyquist plot of the impedance of Cell 1, recorded in the fresh state and at two different aging levels, along with the corresponding fits using both “forward” and “reverse” paths. As observed, the fresh cell exhibits the EIS spectra of a typical semi-infinite diffusion-controlled faradaic reaction described by the Randles like circuit, while a second large semicircle is evident with advanced aging, which gradually forms as seen in the intermediate aging cell [43]. Both “forward” and “reverse” paths achieve good fits when the spectra show a clear separation of the semicircles or a single semicircle. However, as detailed in Fig. 9b, which magnifies the EIS spectra at intermediate aging level, starting from a spectrum with a single semicircle (as for the case of forward path from the fresh cell) does not capture well the initial formation of the second semicircle, leading to less accurate fitting especially at intermediate SOC. Conversely, starting with a clearly distinguished two-semicircles spectrum (as for the case of reverse path from the aged cell) allows for better tracking of the gradual reduction and eventual disappearance of the second semicircle as the cell approaches the fresh state.

This highlights a key principle for the automated fitting strategy. The optimal starting spectrum is determined not by its chronological position, but by the presence of well-defined, separable impedance features. Since the most aged spectrum in a dataset is the most likely to exhibit such distinct characteristics, the “reverse” path provides a practical strategy for robustly initializing the fitting process without manual intervention. Nevertheless, it is important to note that the algorithm's robustness is not strictly dependent on the presence of two perfectly formed semicircles. Even in cases where the impedance spectrum presents a single, broadened semicircle, as demonstrated with the successful fitting of Cell D in Section 4.2.3, the proposed method is still capable of achieving a high-quality fit.

This finding is further supported by the analysis in Fig. 10a and b, which display the evolution of RMSE and
R 2
metrics, across different aging cycles for both paths evaluated at 25% SOC. As shown, the forward path produces slightly lower-quality fits especially when the EIS spectra do not clearly separate the semicircles. In addition, the noticeable transition of RMSE value at a certain aging cycle suggests irregular tracking of circuit parameters. In contrast, the reverse path consistently yields better RMSE and
R 2
values and maintains similar fitting quality across various aging cycles, then providing a more reliable model for tracking the aging process.

The adopted equivalent circuit, which features a double parallel configuration, is particularly well-suited for modelling electrochemical processes that exhibit two distinct time constants. This configuration enables accurate fitting of EIS spectra that present two semicircles, ensuring convergence to consistent circuit parameters regardless of the chosen aging path. The effectiveness of this approach is confirmed by experimental evidence in Fig. 10, where, beyond 150 aging cycles, the fitting results for forward and reverse paths show no significant differences, as indicated by identical RMSE and
R 2
values.

In contrast, EIS spectra displaying only a single semicircle, rather than two well-defined regions, may be better represented using an equivalent circuit with a single parallel component, corresponding to a single dominant time constant. This adaptation would improve fitting accuracy for cases where aging effects do not lead to the formation of a secondary semicircle.

Nonetheless, Circuit B in combination with the fitting method that propagates parameters from aged to fresh cell, has been identified as the most effective configuration for capturing the spectral evolution, and then to track the aging of typical of lithium-ion cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fitting of multiple EIS spectra evolving with aging of cells
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 11 it is shown the evolution of EIS spectra with aging of four cells of the Dataset 1 at reference 100% SOC. Since the cells are paired for each aging profiles, only EIS spectra of odd cells are shown, while all other experimental and fitted EIS spectra related to different SOC levels (75, 50, 25, 0%) and for the other cells of the dataset can be found in Figs. S4–S7.

Cell 1 and Cell 3 are part of a set of four cells that underwent identical cycling processes, including both galvanostatic and potentiostatic charge and discharge cycles, where only the applied charge current has been changed from 1.3C (AP1) to 1.7C (AP2), respectively. Over 275 cycles, these cells experienced a 20% loss in capacity and similar impedance increase. In particular, the analysis of the EIS spectra shows the formation and enlargement of a second distinct semicircle in the impedance spectra.

Cell 5 comes from a pair that has been subjected to higher temperature conditions during cycling (AP3). Like other cells in the dataset, this cell experienced a 20% loss in capacity. However, a more pronounced and clearer evolution in the impedance spectra was observed in significantly fewer cycles (200 cycles). Moreover, the low frequency tail of the spectra (at right side of the spectrum) fades with aging evolution due to a possible shift of the diffusion process towards low frequencies.

Cell 7 is from a pair that was cycled without potentiostatic charge (AP4) resulting in a slow degradation, which leads to 20% capacity loss in about 400 cycles. Despite the lower number of aging cycles, the evolution of the impedance spectra of this cell with aging is less pronounced in comparison to the other cells.

As can be observed from the fitting shown in Fig. 11 and Figs. S3-S7 the proposed algorithm very well fits all the EIS spectra at different aging state and SOC levels despite difference in shape and magnitude of each spectrum. It is worth noting that, as already mentioned, when the diffusion behaviour deviates from the semi-infinite model, the fitting accuracy of the right tail of the spectra decreases. This is clearly observable for EIS spectra of almost all cells at 0% SOC as shown in Fig. S7. The only exceptions are Cells 5 and 6, which do not exhibit the low-frequency diffusion tail at advanced aging states. In this case, due to the limited range of frequencies analysed, the quality of the fit appears to be improved.

These results are also quantitatively confirmed in Table 5 , where both
RMSE ¯
and b)
R 2
¯
are calculated over the selected SOC for each cell. It can be observed outstanding
R 2
¯
> 0.9992 and
RMSE ¯
< 1.062 × 10−4 for all the considered cells at SOC levels higher than 50%. In accordance with the graphical observation of the fitting results, at 0% SOC, it is calculated the highest value of
RMSE ¯
= 2.566 × 10−4 and the lowest value of
R 2
¯
= 0.9982, confirming the minor precision in fitting EIS spectra due to the deviation of diffusion process. Nonetheless, the precision of the method seems to be robust allowing to capture significative difference in EIS shape and hence enabling a proper tracking of lithium-ion cell aging.

Note that, similarly to the proposed method, commercial EIS-fitting packages (e.g. ZView-Scribner, NOVA-Metrohm AG) exploits a nonlinear least square regression to fit the data with the function from the equivalent circuit, using the comparable solvers (e.g., Levenberg–Marquardt algorithm). The fitting procedure will change the values of the parameters until the mathematical function matches the experimental data within a certain error margin. In that sense, one can expect comparable curve-fitting accuracy between their tools and our TRF-based implementation. Beyond accuracy, the proposed method automates the entire workflow by fixing the equivalent-circuit topology, estimating the initial parameters automatically, and performing sequential multi-spectrum fitting. This fully automated pipeline prevents systematic biases from manual initial-guess selection and dramatically reduces human time. On the standard desktop workstation used, fitting a typical single-spectrum made up of 60 frequency points takes around 0.5–0.7 s, with the total processing time increasing in proportion to the size of the dataset (e.g. less than 8 s to fit a dataset consisting of 12 EIS spectra).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fitting of EIS spectra with limited high frequencies and uneven evolution
ce:section.ce:para: As known, the inductive part of the EIS curve in the high-frequency's region, is often affected by measurement set-up, and linked with contact resistance between the cell and probes/holder and cables, and environmental high frequencies noises. This could introduce misleading information, not related to the real behaviour of the battery. For the motivation reported above, higher frequencies are often neglected from the impedance analysis [64]. Moreover, in many practical situations, the evolution of EIS spectra with aging could not be always regular as that reported for Dataset 1. Therefore, it would be interesting to test whether the proposed algorithm can also work in conditions where the inductive part is missing and the EIS spectra evolve unevenly with aging. EIS spectra of Cell C, which are characterized by a limited high frequency range and irregular aging evolution with cycles, were used for this purpose. Results of the fitting for such sample are shown in Fig. 12 for 100% SOC, and in Fig. S8 for other SOCs. Therein, it is possible to appreciate graphically the good fitting between estimated and experimental data despite lack of inductive tail (high frequencies data points) and the irregular evolution of the EIS spectra with aging.

A quantitative assessment of the fitting quality is provided by the average
RMSE ¯
and
R 2
¯
values reported in Table 6 . Despite challenges arising from the absence of the positive portion of the imaginary impedance and the non-uniform evolution of the spectra with aging, the fitting algorithm achieves a sufficiently high level of accuracy. Notably, even under the most challenging condition (0% SOC), where fitting is inherently more difficult, the obtained average values of
R 2
¯
> 0.996 and
RMSE ¯
< 1.473 × 10−3 demonstrate the robustness and reliability of the fitting procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative validation of EIS fitting algorithm
ce:section.ce:para: EIS spectra from a public available dataset provided in [54] were utilized to benchmark the fitting algorithm against the method described in [37]. Both methods employ a similar equivalent circuit, as detailed in Section 2. The primary focus of this comparison is to assess whether updating the optimized parameters from one spectrum to the next, rather than re-initializing the parameters for each spectrum as done in the reference method, results in a more consistent and reliable estimation of the circuit parameters over a series of measurements.

The proposed algorithm was applied to experimental data from Cell D, and the results for thirteen EIS spectra recorded at 100% SOC are presented in Fig. 13 . The result demonstrates excellent agreement between experimental and fitted data, as also evidenced by average
R 2
¯
and
RMSE ¯
values of 0.999 and 1.16 × 10−4, respectively. Notably, as observed in Fig. 13, the EIS spectra exhibit an unusual aging trend. Indeed, the impedance initially decreases before transitioning to the typical behaviour characterized by an increase in the semicircle amplitude and a rightward shift. Moreover, the EIS spectra display always a single semicircular feature, rather than the dual semicircular profiles often observed in other lithium-ion cells with aging evolution. Despite this, the proposed fitting method accurately captures the key features present in the experimental data and reproduces all spectra with high fidelity. This robust performance, even when faced with non-standard spectral evolution, further substantiates the efficacy of the automated fitting algorithm in extracting meaningful circuit parameters from EIS measurements across diverse aging conditions.

Furthermore, the circuit parameters estimated for Cell D with the proposed algorithm were compared to the corresponding parameters obtained using the method described in [37]. Fig. 14 displays all nine equivalent circuit parameters (L, R, R
ct1
, Q
1
, α
1
R
ct2
, Q
2
, α
2
, z
w ) as a function of SOH of the cell (defined as the proportion of available capacity at a given aging stage relative to that at the fresh state). The parameters obtained with the proposed algorithm exhibit similar or more regular trends across the aging process compared to those derived using the reference method, which, in some cases, displays noticeable discontinuities.

These findings indicate that the strategy of propagating optimized parameters from one spectrum to the next leads to a more robust and coherent characterization of the circuit parameters, thereby facilitating a more reliable tracking of battery aging mechanisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Demonstration test case: quantification and analysis of the degradation mechanisms
ce:section.ce:para: To further demonstrate the utility of the fully developed diagnostic tool in tracking and quantifying degradation mechanisms, the algorithm was applied to the entire Dataset 1 and results are online available [53]. In this analysis, the circuit parameters for each of the eight cells (derived from EIS fitting results discussed in Section 4.2.1) and measured across various aging states, were incorporated into the aging model described by Eqs. (34), (35) and (36). This integrated approach allows key degradation phenomena (LAM, LLI and CL) to be evaluated quantitatively, enabling their evolution to be monitored as the cell ages.

Fig. 15 summarizes the evolution with aging cycles of the three degradation modes computed for each cell at five distinct SOC levels. The regular trends observed for LAM, LLI, and CL as a function of cycle number confirm that the fitting algorithm reliably captures the evolving characteristics of the EIS spectra. These consistent trends not only validate the robustness of the fitting algorithm but also provide a reliable basis for analysing the specific degradation mechanisms affecting the cells.

Notably, the results indicate that cells cycled at ambient temperature of 45 °C (Cells 5 and Cell 6) exhibit more pronounced degradation across all three mechanisms. In particular, the LLI mechanism, which is characteristic of SEI formation [10], shows significant growth. In contrast, cells operating under manufacturer-recommended conditions and room temperature (Cells 1 and Cell 2) or cycled at high charge currents (Cells 3 and Cell 4) show similar degradation trends, with overlapping values across all SOC levels. Instead, Cell 7 and Cell 8, having the less severe aging profile, accordingly, shows lower values of LAM, LLI and CL for all SOC and aging cycles. A further analysis of Fig. 15 reveals that for the type of cell under analysis, the quantification of degradation mechanisms is markedly different when evaluated at 100% SOC, compared to the other levels investigated. In particular, the LLI is much more pronounced, which is because the transport phenomena within the passivation layers at the anode and cathode, as well as the charge transfer reactions, are highly sensitive to the SOC level, as supported by a previous finding [65]. At intermediate SOC levels, the overlapping contributions from both electrodes result in a less sensitive differentiation of degradation phenomena, making the quantification less dependent on SOC.

Another observation relates to the early aging cycles, particularly at low state of charge, (notably 0% SOC). In several instances, negative degradation percentages are calculated, not because of errors in the fitting procedure, but due to an effective reduction in the semicircle magnitude during the initial aging cycles (as illustrated in Fig. S9a). As well known, even as-received batteries that have undergone the manufacturer's formation protocol can still experience a rapid evolution of their physical and electrochemical properties in early cycles [66]. This phenomenon can be attributed to a post-formation break-in period. During this phase, changes such as improved electrode wetting and stabilization of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) lead to a transient reduction in impedance, particularly in parameters associated with the semicircle features of the EIS spectrum. For example, cells operated at low depths of charge (such as Cells 7 and Cell 8) exhibit an initial decrease in key equivalent circuit parameters (e.g., R
ct1
, R
ct2
, z
w ) within the first 50 cycles (Fig. S9b). This early-life reduction in impedance, transiently yield negative degradation values during the first 250 cycles, until the EIS spectra evolve beyond their initial configuration as the cells age. Such decreases in impedance, which can be correlated with early break-in phenomena, have been effectively exploited for battery life prediction [67–69]. Despite the inherent variability in the EIS spectra during these initial cycles, the developed algorithm is sufficiently accurate to capture these conditioning trends, thereby offering critical insights into early battery dynamics and informing lifetime prognostics.

In this work, a novel, automated methodology for extracting equivalent circuit parameters from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data has been developed and validated for lithium-ion battery degradation modelling. The proposed approach integrates a robust Python-based tool that combines automatic initial parameter estimation with iterative parameter optimization using a Trust Region Reflective (TRF) least squares algorithm. This methodology addresses challenges associated with conventional EIS fitting techniques, which typically require expert intervention for initial parameter selection and yield inconsistencies when processing large datasets.

The methodology presented in this work has been opportunely calibrated and validated onto two EIS datasets, then a demonstration case has been proposed. The calibration results have confirmed that the automated initial parameter estimation, based on a systematic partition the EIS spectra into high, mid, and low frequency regions using optimized percentile thresholds, significantly enhances the convergence of the fitting algorithm across diverse EIS spectra derived from various lithium-ion cell types. Moreover, it has been revealed that propagating the optimized parameters from one spectrum to the next results in a more coherent and reliable estimation across successive aging cycles. Even when compared with state of the art EIS fitting methods, it produces results in line or superior.

The developed algorithm, which implements an iterative and sequential optimization flow, not only enhances the fitting accuracy but also provides a robust framework for tracking the evolution of system parameters over time. This is particularly useful in aging studies, where parameters may change gradually. Starting each new fit with the optimized parameters from the previous fit can lead to more accurate tracking of these changes. Furthermore, the method has demonstrated its versatility by effectively handling non-regular aging trends, thereby enhancing its potential for practical applications that extend beyond the laboratory test case. The open code release along with this paper allows Researchers to use the proposed method with some guidance provided in SI document or to customize it according to their specific needs (e.g. adding convergence criteria, changing the ECM, etc.). The proposed method is currently intended to be used with pre-processed experimental EIS data. As a future work, its range of applicability could be extended. For instance, in cases where EIS data come from an unknown source, a procedure to evaluate the goodness of input data via Kramers-Kronig transforms or similar techniques could be implemented.

Beyond the fitting procedure, this work integrated the optimized circuit parameters into an aging model that quantitatively evaluates the principal degradation mechanisms (CL, LAM and LLI) of lithium-ion cells. By correlating increases in ohmic resistance (indicative of CL), charge-transfer resistances (reflecting the cumulative effects of LLI), and changes in the Warburg impedance (associated with LAM), the model provides a reliable tool to monitor the evolution of battery degradation as a function of aging.

In summary, the present work advances the state of the art in EIS data interpretation for lithium-ion batteries by delivering an automated, high-accuracy tool that facilitates both the extraction of meaningful circuit parameters and the quantitative assessment of key degradation phenomena. The promising results obtained, not only demonstrate the effectiveness of the method for tracking battery aging but also highlight its potential as a valuable diagnostic and prognostic tool in the context of energy storage systems. Indeed, its ease of integration into advanced battery management systems could make it valuable for both research and practical applications.
